title: The empty bench
summary: A diary entry about a park bench where an old man used to sit every day.
body:
Today, the bench was empty. For four years, an old man with a grey hat sat there every afternoon. He always carried a small paper bag. Inside the bag, there was bread for the birds. He fed them slowly, piece by piece. Sometimes he closed his eyes. Sometimes he smiled at nothing.

I don't know his name. I only saw him from my balcony. I drank my coffee and watched him. He never looked up. He probably didn't know I was there.

Last week, he stopped coming. The bench was empty on Monday, Tuesday and Wednesday. I told myself, 'Maybe he is sick. Maybe he is on holiday.' But somehow, I knew.

Today, two pigeons walked around the bench. They looked confused. They waited. After a while, they flew away. I drank my coffee. It was cold. I thought, 'A bench is not a person. A bench cannot miss anyone.' But it really looked like the bench was missing him.

Tomorrow, I will buy some bread. I will go down to the park. I am not the man with the grey hat. But the birds are still hungry, and the afternoon is still long.

grammarNotes.title: Used to / past habit
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Hành động lặp lại trong quá khứ có thể dùng 'past simple' với trạng từ: 'sat there every afternoon', 'always carried'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Past habits can be shown with the past simple + frequency word: 'sat there every afternoon', 'always carried a small bag'.

blanks.explanation: There was bread for the birds.
She wonders if he is on holiday.
Her coffee was cold (she had been watching, not drinking).
She will go to the park.

translationVi:
Hôm nay, chiếc ghế trống. Suốt bốn năm, một ông cụ đội mũ xám luôn ngồi ở đó mỗi buổi chiều. Ông luôn mang theo một túi giấy nhỏ. Bên trong là bánh mì cho lũ chim. Ông cho chúng ăn chầm chậm, từng mẩu một. Có khi ông nhắm mắt. Có khi ông mỉm cười với khoảng không.

Tôi không biết tên ông. Tôi chỉ nhìn ông từ ban công nhà mình. Tôi uống cà phê và nhìn ông. Ông không bao giờ ngẩng lên. Chắc ông không biết tôi ở đó.

Tuần trước, ông không đến nữa. Ghế trống vào Thứ Hai, Thứ Ba và Thứ Tư. Tôi tự nhủ: 'Có thể ông bị ốm. Có thể ông đi nghỉ.' Nhưng bằng cách nào đó, tôi đã biết.

Hôm nay, hai con bồ câu đi quanh ghế. Trông chúng bối rối. Chúng chờ. Một lúc sau, chúng bay đi. Tôi uống cà phê. Cà phê đã nguội. Tôi nghĩ: 'Một cái ghế đâu phải con người. Cái ghế đâu thể nhớ ai.' Nhưng trông cái ghế thật sự như đang nhớ ông.

Ngày mai, tôi sẽ mua ít bánh mì. Tôi sẽ xuống công viên. Tôi không phải ông đội mũ xám. Nhưng lũ chim vẫn đói, và buổi chiều thì vẫn dài.

questions.prompt: Who used to sit on the bench?
options: An old man with a grey hat
questions.explanation: 'An old man with a grey hat sat there every afternoon.'

questions.prompt: How long had he been coming?
questions.hint: Look for a number of years.

questions.prompt: What was in his paper bag?
questions.explanation: 'Inside the bag, there was bread for the birds.'

questions.prompt: How did the writer watch him?
questions.explanation: 'I only saw him from my balcony.'
questions.hint: Look for a place at home.

questions.prompt: Did the writer know the man's name?
questions.hint: Look for a direct sentence about names.

options: The man moved to another city

questions.prompt: What did the pigeons do today?
options: Walked around and waited, then flew away
questions.hint: Look for two actions of the birds.

questions.prompt: What does the writer feel about the empty bench?
options: Like it is missing the man, even if a bench can't really miss anyone
questions.explanation: 'It really looked like the bench was missing him.'
questions.hint: Look for a sentence about the bench and missing.

questions.prompt: What will the writer do tomorrow?
options: Buy bread and go to the park
questions.explanation: 'Tomorrow, I will buy some bread. I will go down to the park.'

options: When someone we never spoke to disappears, we can quietly take their small kindness forward
Old men should not feed birds
questions.explanation: The writer decides to feed the birds in the man's place.
questions.hint: Why does the writer want to feed the birds?

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer never spoke to the old man, but feels his absence. Have you ever 'known' a stranger only through their daily routine — and what does that say about how we share a city?
